title: Status and Progress CLI

story.i_want_to: query project progress, epic progress, story details, ready stories, and blocked stories via the CLI
story.so_that: I can make informed decisions about what work to assign next and identify bottlenecks in the development pipeline

rationale: Status and progress queries are the most frequently used CLI operations for orchestrators and human developers alike. The orchestrator needs to poll for ready stories to assign, check which stories are blocked, and understand overall project progress. These commands translate complex API queries into simple, composable CLI invocations that agents can parse from JSON output and humans can read in table format.

acceptance_criteria.description: 'loopctl status --project PROJECT' calls GET /api/v1/projects/:id/progress and displays the project-wide progress summary including total stories, stories by agent_status, stories by verified_status, epic completion counts, and overall verification percentage
'loopctl status --epic EPIC_NUM' calls GET /api/v1/epics/:id/progress and displays the epic-level progress with per-story status breakdown

'loopctl status STORY_NUM' calls GET /api/v1/stories/:id and displays full story detail including both agent_status and verified_status, assigned agent, timestamps, dependencies, and latest verification result
'loopctl next --project PROJECT' calls GET /api/v1/stories/ready?project_id=:id and displays stories that are ready to be claimed (all dependencies verified, agent_status=pending)

'loopctl blocked --project PROJECT' calls GET /api/v1/stories/blocked?project_id=:id and displays stories that are waiting on unverified dependencies, including which specific dependencies are blocking them
All status commands respect the --format flag (json, human, csv)
The 'next' command in JSON format returns an array of story objects that agents can directly parse and act on
Story status command resolves STORY_NUM by matching the story number field (e.g., '14.1') within the project scope, requiring --project if multiple projects exist
Epic status command resolves EPIC_NUM by matching the epic number field within the project scope, requiring --project if multiple projects exist

test_cases.name: Status project displays progress summary
preconditions: Config file exists with valid server and api_key

Req.Test mock returns 200 for GET /api/v1/projects/:id/progress with {total_stories: 50, agent_status_counts: {pending: 20, assigned: 5, implementing: 10, reported_done: 15}, verified_status_counts: {unverified: 35, verified: 12, rejected: 3}, epics_complete: 2, epics_total: 8}
expected_results: Output includes total story count (50)
Output includes agent status breakdown
Output includes verified status breakdown
Output includes epic completion (2/8)
Output includes verification percentage (24%)

test_cases.name: Status epic displays per-story breakdown
preconditions: Config file exists with valid server and api_key
Req.Test mock returns 200 for GET /api/v1/epics/:id/progress with epic progress data including per-story statuses
expected_results: Output includes the epic title and number
Output includes each story in the epic with its agent_status and verified_status
Stories are ordered by story number

test_cases.name: Status story displays full detail
preconditions: Config file exists with valid server and api_key
Req.Test mock returns 200 for GET /api/v1/stories/:id with full story data including assigned_agent, dependencies, latest verification
expected_results: Output includes story number, title, description
Output includes agent_status and verified_status
Output includes assigned agent name (if assigned)
Output includes dependency list with their statuses
Output includes latest verification result (if any)

test_cases.name: Next command returns ready stories as JSON array
preconditions: Config file exists with valid server and api_key
Req.Test mock returns 200 for GET /api/v1/stories/ready?project_id=:id with 3 ready stories
expected_results: Output is valid JSON array with 3 story objects
Each story object includes id, number, title, epic_id
Stories are ordered by epic position then story number

test_cases.name: Blocked command shows blocking dependencies
preconditions: Config file exists with valid server and api_key
Req.Test mock returns 200 for GET /api/v1/stories/blocked?project_id=:id with 2 blocked stories including their blocking dependency details
expected_results: Output lists 2 blocked stories
Each blocked story shows which dependencies are not yet verified
Blocking dependencies show their current agent_status and verified_status

test_cases.name: Next command with no ready stories returns empty result
preconditions: Config file exists with valid server and api_key
Req.Test mock returns 200 for GET /api/v1/stories/ready?project_id=:id with empty array
expected_results: JSON format: output is an empty array []
Human format: output displays 'No stories are ready for assignment'

test_cases.name: Status command requires --project flag
expected_results: Returns {:error, message} indicating --project, --epic, or story number is required
No HTTP request is made

technical_notes: Story number resolution: the CLI takes a human-friendly story number like '2.1' and needs to resolve it to a UUID. This requires querying the stories list filtered by project and number, or using a lookup endpoint
Epic number resolution: similar to story numbers, take an integer epic number and resolve to UUID via the epics list endpoint filtered by project
The 'next' command is the primary interface for orchestrator agents to discover assignable work — JSON output must be clean and parseable
Consider caching the project ID lookup (slug to UUID) within a single command invocation to avoid duplicate requests
For human format, use box-drawing characters or simple dashes/pipes for table formatting — keep it readable in terminal output
The blocked command should include enough dependency context that the user can understand what needs to happen to unblock each story

Testing infrastructure: (1) All tests use async: true via DataCase/ConnCase, (2) Mox mocks defined in test/support/mocks.ex, (3) Default permissive stubs set in DataCase/ConnCase setup via stub_all_defaults/0 and Mox.set_mox_from_context(tags) for async isolation, (4) setup :verify_on_exit! in every test module, (5) Fixtures in test/support/fixtures.ex with build/2 (no DB) and fixture/2 (DB insert with auto-dependency resolution), (6) NEVER Application.put_env in tests — all service swapping via config/test.exs.

CLI HTTP client uses Req with Req.Test plug injection for tests. Config: config :loopctl, :api_req_plug, {Req.Test, Loopctl.CLI.Client} in test.exs. CLI client checks Application.get_env(:loopctl, :api_req_plug) and applies plug if present.
